teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the basic question of how different parts of a robot software system communicate.
teaching_arc.progression: Define the core pub-sub objects, show the data flow, then reinforce it with examples and checks.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish with a working communication mental model they can reuse in every later ROS topic.

audience_level: introductory autonomous mobile robotics students learning ROS 2 through lab-first workflows
topic_goal: Build a clear mental model of ROS 2 nodes, topics, publishers, and subscribers before students start wiring systems together.
topic_takeaways: Students should understand ROS 2 as message-passing between nodes.
Students should distinguish the roles of publishers, subscribers, and topics.
Students should be able to trace a simple ROS 2 data flow verbally.
style_notes: Keep the tone hands-on and confidence-building.
Connect every concept to what students will debug in a terminal, simulation, or robot lab.
Use visualization and mental models before deep command details.
Keep the data-flow picture alive throughout the lesson.
Repeat the publisher-topic-subscriber relationship until it feels stable.
transition_style: Move from the mental model to the concrete lab step students will run next, then close each section by explaining what to verify.

slides.narration_seed: Outcome: students can create a workspace, create a package, understand node/graph/communication, build and run a Python talker/listener, and inspect with ROS CLI.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Build a clear mental model of ROS 2 nodes, topics, publishers, and subscribers before students start wiring systems together.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into ROS 2 in 60 seconds so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may memorize ROS 2 terms without seeing the actual data-flow relationship between them.
slides.explanation_style: Frame ROS 2 Basics: Nodes + Communication as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep ROS 2 Basics: Nodes + Communication connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how ROS 2 Basics: Nodes + Communication sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.
slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.

slides.narration_seed: Use ROS 2 in 60 seconds to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: Node = a running program (a process).
Interfaces = message definitions (msg/srv/action).
Communication = Topics / Services / Actions.
ROS tools help you see the graph and debug logically.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from ROS 2 Basics: Nodes + Communication into ROS 2 in 60 seconds so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Terminal Setup: the must-do steps so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use ROS 2 in 60 seconds to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep ROS 2 in 60 seconds connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how ROS 2 in 60 seconds sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Emphasize: package != node. Package is folder/build unit; node is running process.
must_cover: A package contains: code, package.xml, setup.py (Python) or CMake files.
ROS finds executables through the package install + entry points.
We use Python packages today: ament_python + rclpy.
Official: Creating a package
slides.transition_from_previous: Use What is a package? to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once What is a package? is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define What is a package? in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep What is a package? connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain What is a package? accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Explain: std_msgs gives us String message type. rclpy is the python client library.

Check it exists:

slides.narration_seed: Quick rule: package root = build/metadata; inner ros_basics/ folder = Python code. If talker/listener imports fail, they usually placed files in the wrong folder or missed __init__.py.
must_cover: Go to the package root (this folder contains metadata + build files):
package.xml → package metadata + dependencies (ROS uses this to know what you need).
setup.py → Python install + entry points (ros2 run ... comes from here).
resource/ros_basics → small marker file so ROS can discover the package name (ament indexing).
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Package structure (where code goes) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Package structure (where code goes) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Package structure (where code goes) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Package structure (where code goes) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Package structure (where code goes) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: 'You build systems by connecting nodes, not by writing one huge program.'
must_cover: Examples: camera_node, lidar_driver, nav2_controller, talker.
A node usually has: publishers, subscriptions, timers, services, actions.
ROS tools can list nodes and show what they publish/subscribe.
CLI: Understanding nodes
slides.transition_from_previous: Use What is a node? to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: Package vs Node into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: Which statement is correct?
A node is runtime. A package is the build/share unit on disk.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: Package vs Node and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Quiz: Package vs Node without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: Package vs Node into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: Package vs Node question on their own and explain the reasoning.
slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.

slide_ref.source_title: The ROS Graph (visual map of your system)

slides.narration_seed: Explain quickly: circles are nodes; rectangles are topics. Arrows show direction.
must_cover: The ROS graph shows nodes and how they connect.
rqt_graph draws pub/sub connections (very useful in labs).
We use it to answer: “Who publishes this topic?”
Docs: Understanding Topics (rqt_graph)
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Communication types (how nodes talk) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for The ROS Graph (visual map of your system) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep The ROS Graph (visual map of your system) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture The ROS Graph (visual map of your system) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slides.narration_seed: Define Communication types (how nodes talk) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Topics → data stream (publish/subscribe).
Services → quick request/response (call-and-reply).
Actions → long task (goal + feedback + result + cancel).
Rule of thumb: stream → topic, ask → service, long task → action.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Communication types (how nodes talk) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Communication types (how nodes talk) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Communication types (how nodes talk) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.

slides.narration_seed: Explain queue depth=10 in one line: it buffers recent messages if subscriber is slower.
must_cover: Publisher sends messages to a named channel like /chatter or /cmd_vel.
Subscribers receive messages whenever they arrive (callbacks).
Publishers and subscribers do not need to know each other directly.
Message type must match (example: std_msgs/msg/String).
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Topics (Pub/Sub) — what you must know to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Topics (Pub/Sub) — what you must know is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.

slides.narration_seed: Explain: publisher sends messages, subscribers receive. No direct 'reply'.
must_cover: Many-to-many (many publishers and subscribers can exist).
Best for continuous data: sensors, state, velocity commands.
CLI tools you’ll use:
Source: Understanding topics

slides.narration_seed: Define Services — when and why in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Client sends a request → server replies with a response.
Best when you need a single result now (not a stream).
Common examples: reset, clear costmap, trigger something once.
CLI examples:
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Services — when and why to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Services — when and why is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.story_hint: Keep Services — when and why connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Services — when and why accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Key sentence: services are not for continuous streaming.
must_cover: Client sends a request, server returns a response.
Use when you need an answer now (fast).
CLI tools:
Source: Understanding services
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Actions — when and why so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Services = Request/Response (call-and-reply) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.

slides.narration_seed: Define Actions — when and why in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Client sends a goal → server sends feedback → final result.
Actions can be canceled (important for robots).
Common examples: navigation goal, docking, long manipulation tasks.
Docs: Understanding Actions
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Actions — when and why to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Actions — when and why is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Actions — when and why in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Actions — when and why connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Actions — when and why accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Example: 'Navigate to this pose' is an action in Nav2.
must_cover: For long-running tasks (seconds/minutes).
Client sends a goal → server sends feedback → final result.
Actions are cancelable.
Source: Understanding actions
must_say: Closed-loop control can correct itself because it measures what actually happened.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into ROS 2 Interfaces (Msg / Srv / Action) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often describe feedback correctly in words but still miss what disturbances do to open-loop behavior.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Actions = Goal + Feedback + Result (+ Cancel) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Actions = Goal + Feedback + Result (+ Cancel) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.

slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Explain: This is like a 'struct' or 'schema'. If publisher and subscriber don't use the same message type, they can't talk.
must_cover: Message (.msg) → used by Topics (one-way stream). It defines the fields inside each message.
Service (.srv) → used by Services (request/response). It has Request fields and Response fields.
Action (.action) → used by Actions (goal/feedback/result). It defines Goal, Result, and Feedback.
You can inspect any interface on your system with these CLI commands:

listener subscribes to chatter and prints received messages.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: What does the 10 mean? into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: In create_publisher(..., 10) or create_subscription(..., 10), the 10 is best described as…
It is the QoS history depth (queue size), not the publishing rate.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: What does the 10 mean? and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Quiz: What does the 10 mean? without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: What does the 10 mean? into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: What does the 10 mean? question on their own and explain the reasoning.

likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.

slides.scene_hint: Bring in a TurtleBot silhouette with links and joints highlighted so the robot description reads as structure, not just XML.

slides.narration_seed: Tell them: No robot motion needed. Just implement the action timing + prints.
must_cover: Task: Build a simple Action Server + Action Client.
Behavior required:
• Client sends a goal with x, y, z-orientation (any random values).
• Server immediately responds: "Got it" (goal accepted).
must_say: Closed-loop control can correct itself because it measures what actually happened.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Assignment: ROS 2 Action (Goal → Feedback → Result) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Assignment: ROS 2 Action (Goal → Feedback → Result) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students often describe feedback correctly in words but still miss what disturbances do to open-loop behavior.
slides.explanation_style: Define Assignment: ROS 2 Action (Goal → Feedback → Result) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Assignment: ROS 2 Action (Goal → Feedback → Result) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Assignment: ROS 2 Action (Goal → Feedback → Result) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: TF2 explains how robots understand position/frames. URDF describes robot model.
must_cover: ✅ Workspace + package creation
✅ Node vs package vs ROS graph
✅ Topics / Services / Actions (what to use when)
Students should understand ROS 2 as message-passing between nodes.
✅ Python pub/sub talker + listener built and running
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Wrap-up + Next Topics to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Close by restating the destination of the topic: Students should finish with a working communication mental model they can reuse in every later ROS topic.
likely_student_confusion: Students may memorize ROS 2 terms without seeing the actual data-flow relationship between them.
slides.explanation_style: Use Wrap-up + Next Topics as a final self-check so students leave knowing what they should now be able to explain or do.
slides.story_hint: Keep Wrap-up + Next Topics connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave Wrap-up + Next Topics with the main takeaways of the topic compressed into usable memory.
slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.
